speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back to Git Going with GitHub. This is episode 21: Git Authentication. I am Alex, and today we are turning Git Authentication from a list of instructions into a working mental model.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I will stop us whenever the instructions sound simple on paper but might feel different with a keyboard and screen reader.

speaker: ALEX
text: Personal access tokens, SSH keys, credential storage, and commit signing. That is the surface description. Underneath it, we are building judgment: where to focus, what to ignore, and how to verify the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So we are not using the audio as a shortcut around learning. We are using it to make the learning easier to enter.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A good audio lesson gives someone enough context to try the work with confidence, even before they open the written material.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, set the room for us. What are we walking into?

speaker: ALEX
text: SSH Keys & Personal Access Tokens: Audience: This appendix is for contributors who need to configure Git authentication for push access. The next useful detail is concrete: If you're working entirely through the GitHub web interface or GitHub Desktop, you can skip this.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next layer is this. Learning Cards: Using This Authentication Reference has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here are the anchors worth keeping. This appendix has two main paths: Personal Access Token (recommended) and SSH Keys -- jump to your chosen method via heading navigation. Step-by-step instructions are numbered lists -- your screen reader announces "1 of 8," etc. to track progress. The Troubleshooting section near the bottom covers the error messages you are most likely to encounter. Command-line examples are in code blocks with high contrast -- increase zoom to read them comfortably. Each method (PAT vs SSH) is a separate section with its own step-by-step flow. The "Do not" lists use bold text to highlight security warnings.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the one idea that makes the next few steps less mysterious?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside When You Need Authentication: GitHub requires authentication when you.

speaker: ALEX
text: That shows up in the workshop in a few specific ways. Push commits to a repository. Clone a private repository. Access organization repositories with specific permissions. Clone public repositories. View public repositories on GitHub.com. Read issues and pull requests.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels much more doable when you say it as one move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The magic is not speed. The magic is knowing what changed and why it matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now bring the learner back to the room. Anchor this part on Personal Access Token (PAT). A Personal Access Token is a password-like string you generate on GitHub and use instead of your account password when Git asks for credentials.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you say to someone who is already bracing for this to be too much?

speaker: ALEX
text: Pros has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The parts worth keeping in working memory are these. Works on all operating systems. Easy to set up for screen reader users (no command line required). Can be scoped to specific permissions. Easy to revoke if compromised.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid pull request habit is to separate three questions: what changed, why it changed, and what still needs review. That keeps the conversation useful instead of noisy.

speaker: ALEX
text: That matters because of the next idea. Cons has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: On the ground, that means a few things. You have to store it securely. Expires after a set time (you must regenerate).

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I am listening before the workshop starts, what should settle in my mind first?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, SSH Keys is still useful because you generate a key pair on your computer (public + private), upload the public key to GitHub, and Git uses the private key to prove your identity.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the talk moves from concept to action. Pros has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The room should hear these as checkpoints. Once set up, works automatically (no password prompts). More secure than tokens. Never expires.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now it sounds like a workflow instead of a wall of instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is where confidence comes from: not from never getting lost, but from knowing how to recover.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where do you want a learner to place their attention here?

speaker: ALEX
text: Cons has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the details that keep the idea from floating away. Requires command-line setup (less accessible for some screen reader users). Slightly more complex initial configuration.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before the learner moves on. This part earns its place because why this method: It's screen reader accessible through the GitHub web interface, and you can complete it without command-line Git configuration. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the sequence, because order matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Step 1: Generate the Token: The token appears as a long string in a text field. The next useful detail is concrete: Select all (Ctrl+A), copy (Ctrl+C), and paste it into a secure note or password manager.

speaker: ALEX
text: If someone is taking notes, this is the short list. repo - Full control of private repositories (includes public repo access). workflow - Update GitHub Actions workflows (if you'll work with Actions).

speaker: ALEX
text: First, navigate to github.com/settings/tokens. Then, select "Tokens (classic)" from the left sidebar. After that, activate "Generate new token" → Select "Generate new token (classic)". Finally, give it a descriptive name in the Note field: "Workshop Laptop Token". Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How would you walk the room through that step by step?

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Set expiration: 30 days or 60 days (recommended for temporary workshop use). Then: Select scopes. Next: Scroll down and activate "Generate token". Last: CRITICAL: Copy the token immediately - you cannot see it again. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now slow down for the part people usually miss. Options has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part that makes the next action easier. Password manager (1Password, Bitwarden, LastPass) - best option. Encrypted note in your operating system's secure notes. Plain text file in an encrypted folder (temporary only).

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the version that sounds like an instructor, not a manual.

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the pieces that turn the idea into a usable move. Paste it into a document you sync to cloud storage unencrypted. Email it to yourself. Save it in a public GitHub file.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of detail that keeps a screen reader user oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The named thing - the heading, tab, field, branch, or button - is the handhold.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you keep commands from becoming magic words?

speaker: ALEX
text: Anchor this part on Step 3: Use It. The next time Git asks for your password (when you push, pull from a private repo, or clone a private repo). This is the part to say slowly: Windows Git Credential Manager: Windows will remember this token automatically after your first use. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git config --global credential.helper cache. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Setting Up SSH Keys (Alternative Method). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: if you prefer SSH and are comfortable with terminal commands.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next point gives the learner a handle. Do not treat Step 1: Check If You Already Have a Key as decoration. Look for files named id rsa.pub, id ed25519.pub, or similar. That is not trivia. If you see these, you already have a key.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should they understand before typing anything?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Step 2: Generate a New SSH Key is still useful because Git will print output showing where the key was saved. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: It generates two files: id ed25519 (private) and id ed25519.pub (public).

speaker: ALEX
text: A few details make that real. Press Enter to accept the default file location. Enter a passphrase (optional but recommended).

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your-email@example.com". Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the learner do first, second, and then after that?

speaker: ALEX
text: Step 4: Add to GitHub has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Navigate to github.com/settings/keys; Select "New SSH key"; Key type: Authentication Key; and Key: Paste your public key (should start with ssh-ed25519 or ssh-rsa). The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Turn that into a path someone can follow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 2 checks: Select "Add SSH key"; and Confirm with your password or 2FA code. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learner is not behind if they stop there and check the page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Pausing to verify is not a detour; it is how you keep control of the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the safe way to learn from that example?

speaker: ALEX
text: Step 5: Test the Connection has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like ssh -T git@github.com. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should happen before anyone copies and runs it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This part earns its place because when cloning or adding remotes, use SSH URLs instead of HTTPS.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like SSH format; git@github.com:owner/repo.git; Instead of HTTPS; https://github.com/owner/repo.git. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Switching Between HTTPS and SSH. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Switching Between HTTPS and SSH: If you cloned with HTTPS but want to use SSH (or vice versa), update the remote.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Check your current remote. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Check your current remote has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git remote -v. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Switch to SSH. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Switch to SSH has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git remote set-url origin git@github.com:your-username/repo.git. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a useful checkpoint before anyone starts pressing keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Checkpoints turn uncertainty into information.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Switch to HTTPS. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Switch to HTTPS has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git remote set-url origin https://github.com/your-username/repo.git. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What do you want them to do when the plan breaks?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Troubleshooting Authentication has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: That becomes easier when you listen for these cues. Each troubleshooting entry starts with the error message in quotes as an h3 heading -- press 3 to jump between errors. Solutions include terminal commands in code blocks -- switch to Focus Mode before copying them. If your error is not listed here, search the GitHub Docs authentication troubleshooting page. Error messages are displayed as bold h3 headings for easy visual scanning. Solution steps are numbered and include code blocks you can copy directly. If terminal output is hard to read, paste commands into VS Code's integrated terminal which respects your theme settings.

speaker: ALEX
text: Another way to ground it. Do not treat "Authentication failed" when pushing as decoration. Problem: Your token expired or is incorrect. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Solution. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Solution has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Listen for the small confirmations in this list. Generate a new PAT. Clear your credential cache (Windows: Credential Manager; macOS: Keychain; Linux: git credential-cache exit). Try pushing again - Git will ask for credentials.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the part worth saying out loud. Put "Permission denied (publickey)" into plain language. Problem: SSH key not properly set up.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that because it gives people permission to slow down.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is the goal. We want the page to feel explorable, not fragile.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Solution, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful version is not abstract; it sounds like this. Verify your key is added to GitHub: github.com/settings/keys. Check SSH agent is running: ssh-add -l. Add your key to the agent: ssh-add /.ssh/id ed25519.

speaker: ALEX
text: Put that beside the next piece. This part earns its place because problem: SSH doesn't recognize GitHub's host key. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the teaching move inside Solution?

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like ssh-keyscan github.com /.ssh/known hosts. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the ordered workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: Security Best Practices has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Never share your private key or PAT - treat them like passwords. Then: Use scoped PATs - only grant the minimum permissions needed. Next: Set expiration dates on PATs - regenerate periodically. Last: Use a passphrase on SSH keys - adds another layer of security. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Security Best Practices. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Revoke old tokens when you're done with a project or device; and Don't commit tokens or keys to Git - use.gitignore for config files. Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Commit Signing - Verified Badges and Vigilant Mode. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Commit Signing - Verified Badges and Vigilant Mode: when you push commits to GitHub, each commit shows a small badge: Verified or Unverified. That matters in practice: This badge tells anyone viewing the commit history whether the commit was cryptographically signed - proving it came from you and was not tampered with.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the part I would want someone to say out loud while they work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A learner should always know what they are trying to prove before they take the next action.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid Git habit is to know which branch you are on, what changed, and what confirmation you expect before you run the next command.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where confidence starts to build. Anchor this part on Why It Matters. Open source maintainers increasingly require signed commits before merging. This is the part to say slowly: Some repositories enforce this with branch protection rules. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on SSH Signing (simpler - reuses your existing SSH key). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: if you already have an SSH key set up for authentication, you can use it for signing too. The listener should be able to check this: Step 1: Configure Git to use SSH for signing.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is navigate to github.com/settings/ssh. Step two is select "New SSH key". Step three is change "Key type" to "Signing Key" (not Authentication Key). Step four is paste your public key and save. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git config --global gpg.format ssh; git config --global user.signingkey /.ssh/id ed25519.pub; git config --global commit.gpgsign true. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on GPG Signing (traditional method). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: GPG Signing (traditional method) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like gpg --full-generate-key; Choose: RSA and RSA, 4096 bits, never expires; Enter your GitHub email address when prompted. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Step 4: Add to GitHub. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Navigate to github.com/settings/gpg-keys. Then: Select "New GPG key" → paste the exported public key. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Step 5: Configure Git to sign all commits. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Step 5: Configure Git to sign all commits has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git config --global user.signingkey YOUR KEY ID; git config --global commit.gpgsign true. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is less about memorizing and more about noticing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Once the learner can name the move, the interface becomes much less intimidating.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Vigilant Mode. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. GitHub has an optional setting called Vigilant Mode (in Settings → SSH and GPG Keys → Vigilant mode). That is the difference between guessing and knowing: When enabled, GitHub marks all commits from your account as "Unverified" unless they are signed - even commits that were previously shown without a badge.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the thread going. Why some maintainers enable Vigilant Mode has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the lesson becomes something you can check. It makes tampered or spoofed commits immediately obvious. It signals that the repository cares about commit provenance.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on What you see as a contributor. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: What you see as a contributor has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: For a learner, the useful signals are concrete. Every unsigned commit you push will show a yellow "Unverified" badge. This is a visual signal - commits can still be pushed, but maintainers may block the merge.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the moment where the page starts to make sense. Here is the learner-facing version. Workshop recommendation: SSH signing is simpler to set up than GPG and reuses your existing key. Put another way, if you have 10 minutes, configure it before Day 2 - every commit you push to accessibility-agents will show as Verified. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is what that changes in practice. Navigate to the repository's commit history (Code tab → Commits link). Each commit row contains either "Verified" or "Unverified" as a badge element. NVDA/JAWS: the badge is inside the commit row; use ↓ to read through each row and the badge text is read inline. VoiceOver: use VO+Right through the commit row; the badge is read as a button with the text "Verified" (clicking it shows the certificate).

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid navigation habit is to prove where you are before activating controls. Headings, landmarks, and the address bar are not trivia; they are your map.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Recommended approach. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Recommended approach: sSH keys are great for long-term use, but PATs are faster to set up and more accessible for screen reader users during a time-constrained workshop. That matters in practice: Next: Appendix E: Advanced Git Back: Appendix C: Markdown Reference Teaching chapter: Chapter 00: Pre-Workshop Setup.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Generate a Personal Access Token with 30-day expiration; Scope: repo and workflow; Store it in your password manager; and Use it when VS Code or Git asks for a password. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people carry with them after this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carry the map. Know what page or tool you are in, know which action you are taking, and know what confirmation should follow. If the confirmation is missing, pause. That pause is not wasted time; it is professional judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a better way to say it than just follow the steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Steps matter, but understanding wins. That is episode 21. Next in the series is episode 22, where we keep building the same contributor muscles.
